sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 74-year-old teacher, presented with a cough that started approximately one week ago, with worsening symptoms over the last two days.
The cough is productive of yellow sputum.
The patient reports feeling warm but denies fever.
He also reports some general achiness and a little bit of pressure in his sinuses.
He denies shortness of breath, runny nose, sore throat, or sinus pain.
He reports a fall down the stairs this morning, injuring his right knee.
He has a history of hypertension and diabetes.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes.
He is currently taking lisinopril 20mg daily and metformin, but reports not taking his medications as directed.
He denies smoking, alcohol, or drug use.
Review of systems: The patient reports a cough, productive of yellow sputum, and some general achiness.
He denies fever, chills, shortness of breath, runny nose, sore throat, or sinus pain.
Current medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily; metformin (dose and frequency not specified, but patient reports taking it twice a day).
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.4°F, blood pressure is 125/62, heart rate is in the 80s, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient has pain to palpation of the bilateral maxillary sinuses on facial examination.
The nasal exam reveals erythema and edema of the nasal turbinates bilaterally with associated yellow discharge.
The throat exam shows a midline uvula with no erythema or edema or exudate in the peritonsillar space.
The neck examination reveals bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy.
The lung exam reveals coarse rhonchi present bilaterally that clears with cough.
The heart exam shows a regular rate and rhythm with no murmur.
The right knee exam reveals pain to palpation, decreased flexion and extension, ecchymosis to the right medial knee, and no evidence of an effusion.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a viral syndrome with mild bronchitis, a right medial collateral ligament strain, hypertension, and uncontrolled diabetes.
Problem list: Bronchitis; right medial collateral ligament strain; hypertension; diabetes; unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed guaifenesin 600mg twice daily to help cough up phlegm.
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once daily for the right medial collateral ligament strain.
I ordered a right knee x-ray to rule out any bone injury.
I ordered a lipid panel to ensure everything is okay with the patient's lipid profile.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c to ensure the patient's diabetes is under control.
I recommended the patient monitor their sugar intake and report their blood sugars through the patient portal.
Follow-up: I will be in touch with the patient with the results of the ordered tests.
